title: The Empire Strikes Back (radio)
content:
{{Top|rwm|reb|italics=1}} {{Otheruses|Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back}} {{Audiobook |image=[[File:Esb-radio.png]] |title=''The Empire Strikes Back'' |author=[[Brian Daley]]{{HighBridge|url=empirestrikesback.html|text=The Empire Strikes Back}} |narrator=[[Ken Hiller]] |producer=[[John Bos]] |director=[[John Madden]]
with [[Tom Voegeli]] |cover artist= |illustrator= |publisher=*[[National Public Radio]][[Star Wars: The National Public Radio Dramatization|''Star Wars'': The National Public Radio Dramatization]] *[[HighBridge Audio]] |release date=[[February 14]]–[[April 18]], [[1983]]{{ThisWeek|gaaZjNoO5d4|Princess Leia and Han Solo's Wedding, The Book of Boba Fett Finale, and More!|sw_url=video/princess-leia-and-han-solo-s-wedding-the-book-of-boba-fett-finale-and-more}} {{C|radio broadcast}} |rereleased=[[July 16]], [[1993]]{{C|Cassette/Compact disc}} |media type=*Radio broadcast *Cassette{{HighBridge|url=emstrikbac.html|text=''The Empire Strikes Back''}} *Compact disc *Digital download |run time=4 hours and 56 minutes (296 minutes) |discs=5 |cassettes=5 |pages= |isbn=9781565119710 |timeline=[[3 ABY/Legends|3 ABY]][[The Essential Chronology (real-world book)|''The Essential Chronology'']] dates the {{Film|V}} to [[3 ABY/Legends]] which this radio production adapts |series=[[Star Wars radio dramas|''Star Wars'' radio dramas]] |preceded by=[[Star Wars (radio)|''Star Wars'']] |followed by=[[Return of the Jedi (radio)|''Return of the Jedi'']] }} '''''The Empire Strikes Back''''' is a ten-part radio adaptation of the film [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''Star Wars'': Episode V ''The Empire Strikes Back'']]. It is the second of the [[Star Wars radio dramas|''Star Wars'' radio dramatizations]] adapting the [[original trilogy]]. The radio drama first aired on [[National Public Radio]] in the United States on [[February 14]], [[1983]], as part of its program ''[[wikipedia:NPR Playhouse|NPR Playhouse]]''. The drama followed on the success of the first radio series, [[Star Wars (radio)|''Star Wars'']] ([[1981]]), which had broken NPR's records for audience size and engagement.[[Star Wars: The National Public Radio Dramatization|''Star Wars'': The National Public Radio Dramatization]] As with its predecessor, [[George Lucas]] sold the rights for one dollar in a deal that gave NPR access to all the sound effects and music from the film.{{BookCite|author=Brian J. Robb|title=A Brief Guide to Star Wars |publisher=Hachette |date=2012 |isbn=9781780335834}} Most of the leading crew returned from the first drama: scriptwriter [[Brian Daley]], director [[John Madden]], sound engineer [[Tom Voegeli]] (who was also credited as co-director), and casting and production coordinator [[Mel Sahr]]. Nearly all members of the ''Star Wars'' radio cast with roles to reprise returned for ''The Empire Strikes Back'', including film actors [[Mark Hamill]] and [[Anthony Daniels]]. Joining the cast were [[Billy Dee Williams]], who reprised his role as [[Lando Calrissian/Legends|Lando Calrissian]], and [[John Lithgow]], who played [[Yoda/Legends|Yoda]]. ==Plot summary== The radio drama follows the plot of the film ''The Empire Strikes Back'', telling the story of [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]]'s rescue by [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]], the [[Battle of Hoth/Legends|Battle of Hoth]], Luke's training under [[Jedi Master/Legends|Master]] [[Yoda/Legends|Yoda]], and [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader's]] relentless pursuit of Solo, [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Princess Leia]], and Luke, which culminates in their [[Duel on Cloud City/Legends|duel on Cloud City]]. Because of the requirements of audio drama, each episode includes a good deal of additional dialogue to describe action that the films convey visually. A few scenes are original to the radio drama. Like ''Star Wars'', it opens with new material amounting to around half of the first episode, "[[Freedom's Winter]]." The opening scene depicts the destruction of [[Renegade Flight]] and a Rebel supply convoy in the [[Battle of Derra IV]]. After that, the episode depicts the launching of the [[Viper probe droid/Legends|Imperial probe droid]] from the Star Destroyer ''[[Avenger/Legends|Avenger]]'' and the efforts of the [[Alliance to Restore the Republic/Legends|Rebels Alliance]] to establish [[Echo Base/Legends|Echo Base]]. Additional scenes beyond the first episode are mostly brief; they include Luke and Han's night in an [[emergency shelter]] while stranded in the Hoth wasteland; the Rebels' preparations for the Battle of Hoth; Luke reuniting with the pilots of [[Rogue Squadron/Legends|Rogue Squadron]] before leaving for [[Dagobah/Legends|Dagobah]]; and two conversations between Lando Calrissian and [[Lobot/Legends|Lobot]]. ==Development== ===Production=== ''Star Wars'' for radio had comprised 13 weekly episodes—exactly one quarter of a year—to better fit into NPR's calendar,{{WebCite|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/lifestyle/1983/02/22/radio-epics/b7825f23-c394-4ebe-978d-96a30e54d886/ |text=Radio Epics |work=The Washington Post |date=1983.02.22 |author=Richard Harrington |archivedate=20170828090238}} ''The Empire Strikes Back'' was written as a somewhat leaner 10 episodes. Madden directed the recording at A&R Studios in New York City over ten days. For the post-production, Voegli again oversaw the sound mixing to combine the new dialogue with the film's music score and sound effects library. Voegli had access to new equipment not available for the first ''Star Wars'': as the end credits of each episode boast, "Post-production was realized at Studio M in St. Paul on the 3M digital mastering system." While recording the dialogue had taken only 10 days, Voegli's work in St. Paul took 150 days, almost as long as post-production for the film itself. ===Promotion=== NPR again went to considerable effort to promote the radio play, commissioning a movie-like poster using art by [[Ralph McQuarrie]]. A more unusual promotion was commissioning food writer [[Craig Claiborne]] to create a recipe for Yoda's [[Rootleaf stew/Legends|rootleaf stew]], which the Jedi Master served Luke in his hut on Dagobah. The recipe ran in magazines and newspapers across the country just before the series aired.{{WebCite|text=Yoda's Incredible Herb Stew|url=http://www.mkescene.com/forums/eats-and-drinks/yoda's-incredible-herb-stew/?wap2|archive-url=https://archive.today/20150209120846/http://www.mkescene.com/forums/eats-and-drinks/yoda's-incredible-herb-stew/?wap2|archivedate=20200811055631}} NPR celebrated the premiere of the first episode at an event at New York's Hayden Planetarium. ==Release and reception== The first episode, "Freedom's Winter," was first broadcast on February 14, 1983. New episodes aired weekly on Mondays, with the finale "[[The Clash of Lightsabers]]" airing on [[April 18]]; some local stations followed different schedules.{{BookCite|author=Christopher H. Sterling |title=Encyclopedia of Radio |number=vol. 3 |publisher=Routledge |date=2004 |isbn=9781135456498 |link=https://books.google.com/books?id=-UeveLMilioC&dq=Frank%20Mankiewicz%20star%20wars&pg=PA2206 }} The recorded radio dramatization was published by [[HighBridge Audio]]. In 1996 HighBridge released a Collector's Limited Edition of [[Star Wars radio dramas|the entire radio trilogy]]. The radio drama's original script was published in ''[[The Empire Strikes Back: The National Public Radio Dramatization]]'' in [[1995]]. ==Continuity== ''The Empire Strikes Back'' adds some original material to the events of the film on which it is based, though not nearly as much as its predecessor ''Star Wars''. As with the other NPR radio dramas, all material beyond what was in the original film is considered part of the [[Star Wars Legends|Legends]] continuity. Some elements from the radio broadcasts were later referenced in other ''Legends'' stories. The drama's opening scene, depicting the [[Battle of Derra IV]], was shown in flashbacks in the [[1997]] comic [[X-Wing Rogue Squadron 25|''X-Wing Rogue Squadron'' 25]].[[X-Wing Rogue Squadron 25|''X-Wing Rogue Squadron'' 25]] The episode "[[The Millennium Falcon Pursuit]]" introduced Rogue Squadron pilot [[Tarrin Datch/Legends|Tarrin Datch]], whose backstory and role would be developed in later sources.[[Galaxy Guide 3: The Empire Strikes Back (Second Edition)|''Galaxy Guide 3: The Empire Strikes Back'', Second Edition]] ==Media== {{Mediacat|from=1|soundcat=Audio files from The Empire Strikes Back (radio)}} ===Editions=== *[[February 14]]–[[April 18]], [[1983]]; [[National Public Radio]]; Radio broadcast *{{ISBN|1565110005}}; [[July 16]], [[1993]]; [[HighBridge Audio]]; Cassette tape *{{ISBN|9781565110076}}; July 16, 1993; HighBridge Audio; Compact disc *{{ISBN|9781565119710}}; July 16, 1993; HighBridge Audio; Digital download ===Episodes=== {|{{Prettytable}} !
